description:
https://ebird.org/checklist/S152125027https://ebird.org/checklist/S152262766
I was the original finder of the bird(s).   Description from eBird checklist on Day 1:  I initially heard several call notes sounding vaguely like a distant Am Robin. After a short amount of time, I heard the calls again, seemingly getting closer but I saw no robins. It clicked that this could be the bird I was hoping for and flipped on Merlin which hit Sprague's immediately. I strained to get a visual on the bird but saw nothing and unfortunately the bird seemed to moving south and getting fainter until I couldn’t hear it. I continued walking and flushed a pipit (streaky brown, more slender shaped than HOLA or longspurs, a thin, sharp bill and with very white-edged tail) about 25 feet ahead of me. It flew low then up and out of view. It uttered a few of the same sharp calls as the first. No recordings of second bird as I was unsuccessfully attempting to obtain flight photos. Typical behavior and proper habitat for this species. Migration was forecasted to be good for the Front Range today with a front breaking to the north and winds overnight from the east/northeast.  The day before this observation I had observed a small bird flying over calling in similar habitat within the county that gave me pause.  It was left unidentified on that list, but caused me to review flight calls of Sprague's Pipits later that night.  As pipits had been reported in Yuma county already, I decided to go to Coal Creek Open Space that next day with the intent of specifically looking for this species as well as migrating Longspurs (which I knew was a longshot but it worked out!).  Photos obtained on Day 2 show streaky buff pipit with white outertail and slightly brownish auricular area (see eBird reports).
